paragraphs.text: The time was drawing near for Sarah to give birth; she bore Isaac and fed him at the breasts of old age. And after Isaac had been circumcised and weaned, on the day of the “great feast” which “Abraham made… on the day that Isaac was weaned” (Gen 21:8), Sarah saw Ishmael laughing. Noticing that Ishmael resembled his mother in everything, and so concluding that, as she had offended Sarah, so Ishmael would mock Isaac, she thought: “If during my life Ishmael deals so with my son, when I die will he not take a portion of my son’s inheritance, perhaps even take two portions, as the firstborn?” So Sarah was zealous for the rights of her son — she who had had no care for her own rights, when without zeal she gave Hagar to her husband. And that the son of the bondwoman should not seize the inheritance from the son of the freewoman, Sarah “said to Abraham: Cast out this bondwoman and her son” (Gen 21:10). For it is not just for the son of the bondwoman to receive the inheritance with the son of promise. Neither is it becoming in thee to act against God’s will in what He has promised thee, and to make an heir of one whom God has not made an heir. But Abraham did indeed wish to make Ishmael an heir, because in his eyes there was no distinction between his sons. Therefore it is written: “And the thing was very grievous in Abraham’s sight because of his son… And God said unto Abraham… in all that Sarah hath said unto thee, hearken unto her voice; for in Isaac shall thy seed be called. And also the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed. And Abraham rose up early in the morning… and gave Hagar bread and water, and the child, and sent her away. And she departed, and wandered in the wilderness… And the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven, and said to her… God hath heard the voice of the lad… hold him in thine hand: for I will make him a great nation. And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water… and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad to drink” (Gen 21:11–14, 17–19).

After this Abimelech and Phicol his commander say to Abraham: Since it is known to us that God is with thee, and helps thee in wars with kings, and has promised to give thee the land of Canaan, we are afraid that, having destroyed the Canaanites, thou wilt also lay waste the land of Abimelech. And therefore they hasten to make a covenant with Abraham. “And they both made a covenant” (Gen 21:27) with Abraham.
